title: Santpuria Alloys Pvt. Ltd.,Giridih vs Acit, C. C., Dhanbad on 22 May, 2026
body: Santpuria Alloys Pvt. Ltd.,Giridih vs Acit, C. C., Dhanbad on 22 May, 2026 THE INCOME TAX APPELLATE TRIBUNAL - RANCHI BENCH VIRTUAL BENCH AT KOLKATA Before Shri Sonjoy Sarma, Judicial Member and Shri Ratnesh Nandan Sahay, Accountant Member ITA No.386/RAN/2025 Assessment Year: 2013-14 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited..............................................Appellant Manjhiladih, Giridih Jharkhand-815302 [PAN: AAICS4215J] vs. ACIT, Central Circle, Dhanbad................................................Respondent Jharkhand-815302 Appearances by: Shri Devesh Poddar, AR, appeared on behalf of the Appellant. Shri Shadab Ahmed, CIT, DR, appeared on behalf of the Respondent. Date of concluding the hearing : April 16, 2026 Date of pronouncing the order : May 22, 2026 ORDER Per Sonjoy Sarma, Judicial Member: This appeal filed by the assessee is directed against the order dated 04.07.2025 of the Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeal) [hereinafter referred to as the 'Ld. CIT(A)'] passed under Section 250 of the Income- tax Act, 1961 (hereinafter referred to as "the Act") for the assessment year 2013-14. 2. Brief facts of the case are that, assessee is a Company, filed its return of income for the assessment year 2013-14 declaring total income at Rs.43,31,410/- derive from sale of sponge iron and its residuary products. The said return was processed under the provisions of the Act by CPC , Bangalore. In the assessee's group of company, a search and seizure operation u/s 132 of the Act was conducted on 14.12.2012 and on subsequent date in the cases belonging to the Mongia Group. The search proceedings therefrom also carried out in the case of the assessee on 14.12.2022. Subsequently, based upon the enquiry and investigation conducted by the department and information available on record, the ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited Assessing Officer believes that income chargeable to tax had escaped assessment. Accordingly, notice u/s 148 of the Act was issued after obtaining prior approval from the competent authority. Therefore, notice u/s 142(1) of the Act were also issued. Calling for necessary details and explanations. In response to the notice issued u/s 148 of the Act, the assessee filed return of income declaring the same income as originally returned. During the course of assessment proceedings, the Assessing Officer from the analysis and reconciliation on material available on record found that M/s Smart Tradecom Private Limited had filed its return of income for the relevant assessment year declaring loss of Rs.27,227/- However, said company has allegedly invested the sum of Rs.4,75,00,000/- in the assessee company towards share capital and share premium. Accordingly, the Assessing Officer despite having negligible financial strength and return loss the said company has invested huge fund in the assessee's company thereby creating suspicion regarding the genuineness and credit-worthiness of the transaction. The Assessing Officer accordingly found a view that said entity has merely accommodation entry provider and that the assessee had routed with own unaccounted money in the right of share capital and share premium through the said company. Accordingly, the Assessing Officer treated the amount of Rs.4,75,00,000/- as unexplained money u/s 69A of the Act and taxed the same in accordance with the provisions of section 115BBE of the Act. 3. Aggrieved by the above order, assessee preferred an appeal before the Ld. CIT(A), however the Ld. CIT(A) dismissed the appeal of the assessee and sustained the addition made by the Assessing Officer. 4. Dissatisfied with the order of the Ld. CIT(A), the assessee is in appeal before this tribunal. At the time of hearing assessee challenge the 2 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited legal issue of reopening under Section 148 of the Act stating that the reopening itself is bad in law. The Ld AR submitted that the reasons recorded in the notice do not specify under which charging provision of section 68 , 69 , 69A, 69B etc. of the Act is alleged to have escaped assessment. The Assessing Officer merely referred to section 149(1)(b)(iii) relating to entries in books of account. It was argued that each of these provisions has distinct statutory requirements, different burdens of proof, and different evidentiary standards. Failure to specify the exact charging provision renders the reopening vague and legally unsustainable, as the assessee is deprived of an effective opportunity to defend itself in view of the Hon'ble Jurisdictional High Court as in the case of Pasari Casting And Rolling Mills (P) Ltd. vs. ITO in 463 ITR 469 (Jharkhand). Besides that it was further contended that the approval under section 151 of the Act granted by the learned DGIT (Investigation), Patna, was mechanical and without application of mind. He further stated that in the instant case of the assessee, the Assessing Officer sought approval from the DGIT (Investigation), Patna, whereas approval u/s 151 of the Act was issued by PCIT, Patna, who had no jurisdiction to grant such recommendation, therefore, the mandatory requirement prescribed under the Act for obtaining valid sanction from the competent jurisdictional authority was not complied with, rendering the initiation of proceedings u/s 147/148 of the Act is invalid in law, which clearly establishes that non-application of mind on the part of issuing authority rendering the approval invalid in law. He also stated that addition is originally made by the Assessing Officer u/s 69A of the Act. However, the Ld. CIT(A) without issuing any proper notice for granting adequate opportunity of hearing to the assessee, converted the addition from section 69A to 68 of the Act. Such conversion of the charging provisions without affording reasonable opportunity to the assessee is contrary to the principles of natural justice and beyond the scope of the appellate 3 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited jurisdiction. Accordingly, the addition sustained is unsustainable in law and liable to be deleted. 5. On merits, it was submitted that M/s Smart Tradecomm Private Limited is a group company of the assessee, having common directors and management since Assessment Year 2011-12. The share applicant was a pre-existing shareholder, and similar share capital received in earlier years had been accepted by the Department. In present case of the assessee no fresh funds were introduced in the share applicant company during the impugned years. The investment was made by liquidation of earlier investments, which is clearly reflected in the audited balance sheets. The Ld. AR further added that all transactions were carried out through banking channels, duly reflected in the audited books of account of both companies, and statutory compliances with the Registrar of Companies were duly made. In the case of the assessee, the Assessing Officer conducted no independent enquiry and merely relied upon the investigation report, therefore the entire assessment order is bad in law. Moreover, there is no allegation that Smart Tradecomm Pvt. Ltd. received funds from shell companies during the year, or the assessee routed its own unaccounted money back to itself. He further added that the principle of consistency squarely applies, as there is no change in facts. The ld. AR submitted the following written submissions: Written Synopsis/ Points in Dispute: - 1. That the search & seizure operation was conducted upon the assessee group on 14/12/2022, subsequent to which proceedings U/s 148 was initiated against the assessee in the impugned year. 2. That the issue involved in the appeal is with respect to the share capital money received by the assessee company from M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd for Rs.4,75,00,000/- in AY 2013-14 against allotment of 950000 shares at a face value of Rs. 10/- and premium of Rs. 40/- 3. That at the very outset, we challenge the proceedings being initiated U/s 148 to the extent that the reasons recorded were vague and did not specify the charge/ section under which it has been alleged that there has been 4 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited an escapement of income. The copy of the reasons recorded along with approval U/s 151 for AY 2013-14 is attached herewith at Page 13-22. 4. That from the reasons recorded, it can be seen that the assessing officer while alleging an escapement of income has only referred to section 149(1)(b)(iii) which refers to entry in the books of account, however does not specify under which provision of section 68 , 69 ,69A,69B etc etc has the income escaped assessment. We quote the decision of Hon'ble Jurisdictional High Court in the case of Pasari Casting and Rolling Mills (P.) Ltd. vs. Income-tax Department [2024] 159 taxmann.com 675 (Jharkhand)[2024] 463 ITR 469 (Jharkhand)[25-01-2024] which has held as follows:-(Copy of the order is attached herewith at Page 39-48) 18. As stated herein above that the recorded reason/impugned Assessment Order is silent under which provision of the Act the additions are sought to be made. It is well settled that the reasons cannot be supplemented by assessment Order or Affidavit. The recorded reason is totally silent whether the amount sought to be taxed is "income of the Petitioner and whether the addition is sought to be made on account of Cash Credit ( Section 68 ), Unexplained Investments ( Section 69 ), Unexplained Money (Section 69A), Amount of Investment, etc. not fully disclosed in books of account (Section 69B), Unexplained Expenditure, etc. (Section 69C). The requirement of each of the aforesaid sections are different and the rules of evidence and burden of proof are also different, hence, unless the Petitioner to put the notice as to the exact contravention or provisions of law under which assessment or additions are sought to be made. the Petitioner cannot defend his case. In the case of Oryx Fisheries (P.) Ltd. v. UOI [2010] 13 SCC 427, it is held by the Hon'ble Supreme Court that the show cause notice should give the notice a reasonable opportunity of making objections against proposed charges indicated in the notice and the person proceeded against must be told the charges against him so that he can make his defence and 5 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited prove his innocence. In the entire course of the proceeding, at no stage the Petitioner is made aware of the provisions of law which have been contravened and/or under which the additions are sought to be made which is in gross violation of the principles of natural justice and the procedure adopted by the Department is not fair or proper In the case of New Delhi Television Lid. v. Dy. CIT [2020] 116 taxmann.com 151/271 Taxman 1/424 ITR 607 (SC), it is held by the Hon'ble Apex Court that the Assessee must be put to notice of all the provisions on which the Department relies. 19. In view of the aforesaid discussions and several judicial pronouncements in the facts and circumstances of this case we are having no hesitation in holding that in the instant case the belief formed by the Assessing Officer suffers from lack of bona fides, is vague, far-fetched, irrelevant, based on conjecture and surmises and also arbitrary and irrational. Further, since the very initiation of the proceedings is bad in law and attracts jurisdictional issue which goes to the root of the case thus we are having no hesitation in holding that the writ is maintainable and the judgments cited by the Revenue has no application in the facts and circumstances of this case As such, quoting the above, it can be concluded that the reasons recorded was vague and that no proper opportunity was allowed to the assessee specifying the charges on which the case has been reopened and as such, the assessment framed thereby in consequent to such reasons is fit to be quashed. 5. That further from the reason recorded and approval U/s 151, copy of which is attached herewith, we would like to state that the designated authorities have only given their recommendation for issuance of notice U/s 148 and not the approval. The terms recommendation & approval are dissimilar, distinct and poles apart and cannot be considered to be as a 6 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited substitute for each other. The law is well settled that the words of a statue are to be interpreted in its literal sense. The word recommendations means: https://www.lexisnexis.co.uk/legal/glossary/recommendations Recommendations are non-binding instruments which do not have any legal force but are merely persuasive. https://thelawdictionary.org/recommend/ a term that means to advise, to suggest, to counsel or to speak in favour of https://dictionary.cambridge.org/dictionary/english/recommendat ion a suggestion that a particular purpose or job something is good or suitable for 6. That we would like to quote the following decision of Hon'ble Apex Court with respect to the rule of interpretation Hon'ble Apex Court in the case of B. Premanand & Others Vs Mohan Koikal & Others Civil Appeal No. 2684 of 2007 dated 16th March 2011 wherein, the Hon'ble Court while adhering to the rule of interpretation at various place has held as follows:- It may be mentioned in this connection that the first and foremost principle of interpretation of a statute in every system of interpretation is the literal rule of interpretation. The other rules of interpretation e.g. the mischief rule, purposive interpretation etc. can only be resorted to when the plain words of a statute are ambiguous or lead to no intelligible results or if read literally would nullify the very object of the statute. Where the words of a statute are absolutely clear and unambiguous, recourse cannot be had to the principles of interpretation other than the literal rule, vide Swedish Match AB vs. Securities and Exchange Board, India , AIR 2004 SC 4219. As held in Prakash Nath Khanna vs. C.L.T. 2004 (9) SCC 686, the language employed in a statute is the determinative factor of the legislative intent. The legislature is presumed to have made no mistake. The presumption is that it intended to say what it has said. Assuming there is a defect or an omission in the words used by the legislature, the Court 7 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited cannot correct or make up the deficiency. vide Delhi Financial Corporation vs. Rajiv Anand 2004 (11) SCC 625. Where the legislative intent is clear from the language, the Court should give effect to it, vide Government of Andhra Pradesh vs. Road Rollers Owners Welfare Association 2004(6) SCC 210, and the Court should not seek to amend the law in the garb of interpretation. As observed by Lord Granworth in Grundy v. Pinniger, (1852) 1 LJ Ch 405: ""To adhere as closely as possible to the literal meaning of the words used, is a cardinal rule from which if we depart we launch into a sea of difficulties which it is not easy to fathom." In other words, once we depart from the literal rule, then any number of interpretations can be put to a statutory provision, each Judge having a free play to put his own interpretation as he likes. This would be destructive of judicial discipline, and also the basic principle in a democracy that it is not for the Judge to legislate as that is the task of the elected representatives of the people. Even if the literal interpretation results in hardship or inconvenience. it has to be followed (see G.P. Singh's Principles of Statutory Interpretations, 9th Edn. pp 45-49). Hence departure from the literal rule should only be done in very rare cases, and ordinarily there should be judicial restraint in this connection. As observed by this Court in CIT vs. Keshab Chandra Mandal , AIR 1950 SC 265 "Hardship or inconvenience cannot alter the meaning of the language employed by the Legislature if such meaning is clear on the face of-the statute" In Union of India and another vs. Hansoli Devi and others 2002(7) SCC (vide para 9), this Court observed: "It is a cardinal principle of construction of a statute that when the language of the statute is plain and unambiguous, then the court must give effect to the words used in the statute and it would not be open to the courts to adopt a hypothetical construction on the 8 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited grounds that such construction is more consistent with the alleged object and policy of the Act." As such, we would like to state that there was no valid approval of the competent authority and that it was merely a recommendation to do an act leaving it on the discretion of the Assessing Officer. This defect is not curable U/s 292BC since there was no approval at all. As such, the proceedings initiated is ab-initio void and assessment framed thereby is fit to be quashed. 7. That coming to the merits of the additions made, as stated above, it is not in dispute that the assessee allotted 950000 shares at a face value of Rs. 10/- and premium of Rs. 40/- to M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd for which Form 2 along with board resolution and copy of share certificate is attached herewith at Page 23-29. 8. That at the very outset, we would like to point out certain facts pertaining to the share applicant company M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd which is fact on record and is undisputed:- • That the assessee group took over the directorship of this company with effect from AY 2011-12 itself and as such, in the year under consideration this company was the group company of the assessee ie they had common directors and management looking after its business. • That from the annexure to the Balance Sheet of M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd for AY 2013-14, copy of which is at Page 3538, it can be seen that 100% shares of this applicant company were held by the director and family members. (Page 37) • That there has been no inducement of any fresh fund in the share applicant company during these 2 impugned years i.e. the capital or the funds/ reserve & surplus of the share applicant which they invested in the assessee company during these years stood accepted in the hands of the share applicant company at its receipt in the previous years. The copy of the audited accounts of the share applicant is attached herewith and the relevant balance sheet is at Page 35 which rather shows that there is a marginal reduction in Investment in Equity Instruments even after including the shares of the assessee. This justifies that substantial investments have been sold by the share applicant doing the year so as to make an investment in the assessee company which justifies the source. 9 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited 9. That the very foundation of the AO is based on the allegation that the investment of funds in M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd in AY 2010-11 was through alleged shell companies and as such the funds invested in the assessee company during these impugned years are alleged to be bogus. However the fact remains that same has no relevance to the case of the assessee for the years under consideration. Moreover it is not even the case of the revenue that M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd had received money from shell companies during the year under consideration which has been invested in the assessee company nor it is established that the assessee has routed back its own unaccounted money. 10. That further we would like to mention that M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd was already our existing share holder from the previous years as well which can be verified from the annexure to our audited balance sheet copy of which is at Page 32. It can be seen that as of previous year ending March 2012, M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd held 96000 shares whereas as on March 2013 M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd held 1046000 shares. Since the shares allotted to M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd have been accepted in the previous year, the same cannot be disallowed during these impugned years holding the applicant to be a shell company. 11. That though principle of res-judicata does not apply to the Tax proceedings yet, one cannot leave aside the "Principle of Consistency", which requires that when the facts & circumstances continue to remain the same, then there should not be any variation in the treatment from earlier year. The Hon'ble Supreme Court has held in several cases including M.M. Ipoh & Ors. vs. CIT (1968) 67 ITR 106 (SC) that: The doctrine of res judicata does not apply so as to make a decision on a question of fact or law in a proceeding for assessment in one year binding in another year. At the same time, it is equally true that the principle of consistency has also been advocated by several Hon'ble courts including the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Radhasoami Satsang vs. CIT (1992) 193 ITR 321 (SC) and the Hon'ble Bombay High Court in CIT vs. Arthur Andersen & Co. (2009) 318 ITR 229 (Bom) by holding that the decision made in earlier years is binding in subsequent years and should be followed. From the above decisions, it follows that a delicate balance needs to be maintained between the principle of consistency depending upon the facts and the governing legal position prevailing in each case. 10 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited Thus since the share application money received by the assessee company from M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd has been accepted in the earlier years and that there is no change in the nature of transaction during the year nor any specific adverse finding is drawn by the AO to allege any accommodation entry, it is submitted that the addition made as alleged bogus share capital U/s 68 is uncalled for and fit to be deleted. 12. That further from the order of assessment it can be seen that the Ld AO has not conducted any independent enquiry or verification and that the Ld AO has been much carried away by the report of the investigation unit while reopening & framing the assessment, which has been arbitrary confirmed by Ld CIT(A). It is not in dispute that the share transactions were done through banking channel and that the details of the same were on record and has never been disbelieved by any authority below. We rely upon the Hon'ble Jurisdictional High Court in the case of CIT Vs M/s Sidhi Vinayak Metcon Ltd in TA No. 39 of 2009 vide order dated 05/04/2018 has held as under:- (Copy of the order is attached herewith at Page 49-51) 5. Having heard counsels appearing for both sides and looking to the facts and circumstances of the case, we see no reason to entertain this Tax Appeal mainly for the reason that the addition made by the Assessing Officer should not have been made by him because amount received towards the share applications was by way of cheque. All the details regarding applicants were furnished by the assessee and Department could have issued notices to such applicants. Hence, there was no need for the Assessing Officer to make addition of Rs. 95,15,000/- 6. It appears that before the Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) also such prayer could have been made by the Department for issuance of Notices upon share applicants. No such application was preferred before C.LT (Appeals) by this appellant. Hence, we see no reason at this stage to remand the matter to the Assessing Officer for further verification of share applications. 7. It appears that similar type of cases are coming to this court often and most of the time same type of error is committed by the Assessing Officers, either deliberately or due to "Induced ignorance". This is not the first time such type of matter has been taken up by this Court 11 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited It is now high time for the Commissioner, Income Tax to have orientation courses or induction courses conducted for the Assessing Officers to make them understand that whenever assessee receives any amount by cheque, there is a need for the Assessing Officer to give notice to the drawers of those cheques 13. That we would even like to state that the Ld AO has vaguely alleged M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd to be a shell company whereas the fact remains neither there is any statement of any entry operator nor does the name of the company flagged in the list of shell companies prepared by the Investigation Unit. As stated above, M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd is a group company of the assessee and was already our pre-existing share holder from the previous year. 14. That the undisputed fact is that the assessee has received share capital money from its group company complete details of which were available on record and without pointing out any specific defect to the same, the Ld AO has made the addition in a vague manner which has been confirmed by Ld CIT(A). The provisions of section 68 is not applicable in the case of the assessee to the extent that:- • Identity cannot be doubted as the share applicant is a group company controlled and managed by the same management. Moreover, the share applicant was already our pre-existing share holder. • Genuineness of the transaction also cannot be doubted since the transactions are through banking channel duly reflected in the audited books of the assessee as well as share applicant, along with necessary compliances to the RoC.. • The Ld AO has himself stated that there was investment made in the share applicant company sometime in 2010. The said investment in the hands of the recipient being the share applicant company was accepted and that the same has been liquidated and invested in the assessee company over the years. As such, the creditworthiness of the share applicant company also stands explained. As such, we would like to submit that the provisions of section 68 is not applicable in case of the assessee 15. That further from the order of assessment at Page 4 onwards it can be seen that on behalf of the assessee all relevant documents so as to establish the 12 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited genuineness of the share transactions were filed before the AO. The AO has simply brushed aside the documents filed by the assessee and we say so because the AO without even pointing any specific defect or whispering about the contents of the documents filed, was much influenced by the report of the investigation unit and has made the addition. No independent enquiry was conducted by the AO during the course of assessment proceedings. 16. That further from the audited accounts of the assessee for the impugned years, copy of which is at Page 31 it can be seen that the financials therein reflects the reasons and need for such share capital raised by the assessee. It can be seen that the turnover of the assessee which was 64 Crore in March 2012 has increased to 86 Crore in March 2013. As such, on perusal of the financials of the assessee company, the share capital money along with premium received from its group company stands justified and no addition U/s 68 was called for. 17. That to support our case on merits and applicability of the provisions of section 68 , we rely upon the following case laws:- a) Principal Commissioner of Income Tax v. Himachal Fibers Ltd - [2018] 98 taxmann.com 173 (SC) SLP Dismissed - Hon'ble Apex Court while upholding the decision of the decision of Hon'ble Delhi High Court has concluded as below:- (Copy of the order is attached herewith at Page 52-54) Cash credit (Share capital) In course of assessment. Assessing Officer noted that assessee was a sick company during relevant year but had nevertheless collected substantial amounts to extent of Rs. 12 crores invested by two share applicants Assessing Officer took a view that identity of shareholders and genuineness of transaction had not been established and, accordingly, brought to tax said amount -Tribunal as well High Court found that even though assessee was a sick company earlier, yet it pulled out of woods in year 2010 - It was also noticed that identity of share applicants was clearly revealed but Assessing Officer did not conduct any enquiry except resting his conclusions on surmises. Accordingly, addition made by Assessing Officer was deleted - 13 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited Whether on facts, SLP filed against order passed by High Court was to be dismissed-Held, yes [Para 2] [In favour of assessee) b) Principal Commissioner of Income-tax Central vs. Pearl Tracom (P.) Ltd. [2025] 176 taxmann.com 284 (Calcutta) [01-07-2025] wherein it has been held as follows:- 6. As noted above, the order passed pursuant to the second revisional order under section 263 has been quashed by the learned Tribunal which has been upheld by this Court. The reasons to believe for reopening the assessment has been recorded by the assessing officer and on going through it we find that the name of the assessee does not feature in the body of the reasons but the assessee has been shown as a beneficiary in the reasons for reopening in paragraph 'details of enquiries made'. There is no specific allegation as against the assessee and as to how layering of funds had taken place. This aspect of the matter was considered by the learned Tribunal and found that the assessing officer never disclosed the details of layers through which the alleged money has been reached into bank account of the assessee nor any information was shared in respect of any documentary evidence for the alleged transaction of accommodation entry for which the assessee was stated to be a beneficiary. This finding of the learned Tribunal is well justified. Apart from that we also find that no specific reasons have been mentioned by the assessing officer as to the nature of the transaction of accommodation entry qua the assessee and this aspect was examined by the learned Tribunal and it was observed that the assessing officer does not disclosed as to whether it is an income or expenses or an allowance or share capital or loan, etc. The factual aspect of the regards the share capital and the share premium was examined by the assessing officer in detail and the documents and details furnished by the assessee were accepted while passing the assessment order dated 30.12.2016. The attempt to revise the said order by way of a second revisional order under section 263 proved futile as the assessee was successful before the Tribunal as well as before this Court. Thus, the learned Tribunal on considering the factual position had rightly granted relief to the 14 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited assessee. The court took note of the factual position and also the decision of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Income-tax Officer v. Lakhmani Mewal Das [1976] 103 ITR 437 (SC) wherein the Hon'ble Supreme Court held that the reasons for the formation of the belief must have a rational connection with or relevant bearing on the formation of the belief. Rational connection postulates that there must be a direct nexus or live link between the material coming to the notice of the Income-tax Officer and the formation of his belief that there has been escapement of the income of the assessee from assessment in the particular year because of his failure to disclose fully and truly all material facts. c) Principal Commissioner of Income-tax vs. Sreeleathers [2022] 143 taxmann.com 435 (Calcutta) [14-07-2022)- (Copy of the decision is attached at Page 58-63) Further to be noted that where the assessee furnishes full details regarding the creditors, it is up to the department to pursue the matter further to locate those creditors and examine their creditworthiness. The assessee responded to the show cause notice and submitted the reply. The documents annexed to the reply were classified under 3 categories namely: to establish the identity of the lender, to prove the genuineness of the transactions and to establish the creditworthiness of the lender. The Assessing Officer has brushed aside these documents and in a very casual manner has stated that mere filing PAN details. balance sheet does not absolve the assessee from his responsibility of proving the nature of transaction. There is no discussion by the Assessing Officer on the correctness of the stand taken by the assessee. Thus, going by the records placed by the assessee, it could be safely held that the assessee has discharged his initial burden and the burden shifts on the Assessing Officer to enquire further into the matter which he failed to do. It is not enough for the Assessing Officer to say so but he should record reasons in writing as to why the documents which were filed 15 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited by the assessee along with the reply does not go to establish the identity of the lender or prove the genuineness of the transaction or establish the creditworthiness of the lender. Where Assessing Officer issued a show cause notice to assessee-company on ground that it had received unsecured loans from alleged bogus company and subsequently passed an assessment order without bringing on record any reason in writing as to why documentary evidences furnished by assessee failed to prove genuineness of said transaction and why it did not establish identity of lender, such impugned assessment order passed being utterly perverse was liable to be quashed. d) Principal Commissioner of Income-tax vs. Ambe Tex Fab (India) (P.) Ltd. [2025] 173 taxmann.com 392 (Calcutta) [07-03-2025] - The Hon'ble Kolkata High Court upholding the detailed order of the Hon'ble Tribunal has held as follows:- (copy of the order is attached at Page 55-57) The issue which falls for consideration is whether the Tribunal was justified in setting aside the order passed by the Commissioner (Appeals) by which the Commissioner (Appeals) affirmed the order passed by the Assessing Officer under section 143(3) . The Tribunal has elaborately considered the factual position and faulted the manner in which the assessment was completed by observing that it was a cryptic order without discussing the facts of the matter nor the submissions made by the assessee nor the documents produced by the assessee to prove the three factors, namely, identity, genuineness of the transactions and creditworthiness of the subscribers. The Tribunal also found that though such documents were once again produced before the Commissioner (Appeals), but the same were not referred to nor any defect or discrepancy was pointed out under the said documents. Apart from that, the Tribunal has also pointed out that there are factual mistakes by the Commissioner (Appeals) while passing the order which are contrary to the conclusion arrived at by the Assessing Officer. The Tribunal took note of the decision of the Supreme Court in Pr. CIT (Central)-1 v. NRA Iron & Steel (P.) Ltd. [2019] 103 taxmann.com 48/262 16 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited Taxman 74/412 ITR 161 (SC) and noted that the principles which were summed up in the said decision when a case is considered under section 68 . After noting the said decision, the Tribunal examined the factual position and found that the initial burden casted upon the assessee has been discharged inasmuch as the assessee had produced the documents to prove the identity of the subscribers, the genuineness of the transaction and creditworthiness of the subscribers. If such was the factual position then in terms of the order passed by the Supreme Court, the burden shifts on the department to prove the contrary. For doing so, it is necessary that the Assessing Officer should embark upon a fact findings exercise which, unfortunately, the Assessing Officer did not do in the instant case. The revenue cannot deny the fact that the order of assessment, as observed by the Tribunal, is a cryptic order. Thus, no question of law, much less substantial questions of law, arising for consideration in this appeal. Accordingly, the appeal fails and is dismissed. [Para 5] e) Income-tax Officer vs. Cornerstone Property Investments (P .) Ltd. [2024] 164 taxmann.com 464 (SC)[15-07-2024]- SLP dismissed against order of High Court that where assessee allotted shares at premium to a company and assessee produced various documents including copies of investor's bank statements. copies of share certificates which showed that it had huge funds, and further, transfer of funds was made through bank, assessee had proved identity and creditworthiness of investor and genuineness of transactions and, thus, no addition under section 68 could be made on account of share premium received by assessee. 18. To Conclude, we would like to submit as under:- • Proceedings initiated U/s 148 was itself bad and void since the reasons recorded was vague and did not specify the charge against the assessee for escapement of income. • That there is no approval U/s 151 of the competent authority and that the recommendation cannot be interpreted as approval. 17 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited • The provision of section 68 is not applicable in case of the assessee since the share applicant is a group company and a pre-existing share holder from the previous years. • On behalf of the assessee, all the relevant documents have been filed to justify the 3 limbs of section 68 . No adverse comment has been made by the lower authorities on the same. Moreover no independent enquiry was conducted by the lower authorities and that the addition has been made in a summary manner relying upon some report from the investigation unit. • As such, the order of assessment is fit to be set aside/ quashed and the addition made thereby for Rs. 4,75,00,000/- in AY 2013-14 is fit to be deleted. • We shall be obliged for your kind consideration and necessary orders on the above". 6. On the other hand, the Ld. DR supported the decision of the authority below. 7. We, after hearing the rival submission of the parties and perusing the material available on record, find that the reasons recorded do not specify the charging provision under which income is alleged to have escaped assessment. On this point, we find reliance in the decision of Hon'ble Jurisdictional High Court in the case of Pasari Casting and Rolling Mills (P.) Ltd. vs. Income-tax Department [2024] 159 taxmann.com 675 (Jharkhand)/[2024] 463 ITR 469 (Jharkhand)[25-01- 2024] wherein it was held as follows:- "18. As stated herein above that the recorded reason/impugned Assessment Order is silent under which provision of the Act the additions are sought to be made. It is well settled that the reasons cannot be supplemented by assessment Order or Affidavit. The recorded reason is totally silent whether the amount sought to be taxed is 'income' of the Petitioner and whether the addition is sought to be made on account of Cash Credit ( Section 68 ), Unexplained Investments ( Section 69 ), Unexplained Money (Section 69A), Amount of Investment, etc. not fully disclosed in books of account (Section 69B), Unexplained Expenditure, etc. (Section 69C). The requirement of each of the aforesaid sections are different and the rules of evidence 18 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited and burden of proof are also different, hence, unless the Petitioner to put the notice as to the exact contravention or provisions of law under which assessment or additions are sought to be made, the Petitioner cannot defend his case. In the case of Oryx Fisheries (P.) Ltd. v. UOI [2010] 13 SCC 427, it is held by the Hon'ble Supreme Court that the show cause notice should give the noticee a reasonable opportunity of making objections against proposed charges indicated in the notice and the person proceeded against must be told the charges against him so that he can make his defense and prove his innocence. In the entire course of the proceeding, at no stage the Petitioner is made aware of the provisions of law which have been contravened and/or under which the additions are sought to be made which is in gross violation of the principles of natural justice and the procedure adopted by the Department is not fair or proper. In the case of New Delhi Television Ltd. v. Dy. CIT [2020] 116 taxmann.com 151/271 Taxman 1/424 ITR 607 (SC), it is held by the Hon'ble Apex Court that the Assessee must be put to notice of all the provisions on which the Department relies. 19. In view of the aforesaid discussions and several judicial pronouncements in the facts and circumstances of this case we are having no hesitation in holding that in the instant case the belief formed by the Assessing Officer suffers from lack of bona fides, is vague, far-fetched, irrelevant, based on conjecture and surmises and also arbitrary and irrational. Further, since the very initiation of the proceedings is bad in law and attracts jurisdictional issue which goes to the root of the case; thus we are having no hesitation in holding that the writ is maintainable and the judgments cited by the Revenue has no application in the facts and circumstances of this case. 7.1 We find that in the case of the assessee, the reasons recorded was vague and that no proper opportunity was allowed to the assessee specifying the charges on which the case has been reopened. Accordingly, on this legal point, we allow the ground of the assessee following the above decision of the Hon'ble Jurisdictional High Court. 7.2 Further, we observed that the validity of the reassessment proceedings initiated under Sections 147 / 148 of the Income-tax Act, 1961 has been seriously challenged by the assessee on the ground that there was no valid approval as contemplated under Section 151 of the Act. On perusal of the reasons recorded as well as the sanction accorded by the designated authority, it is evident that the competent authority merely recorded a "recommendation" for issuance of notice under Section 148 of the Act and did not grant any categorical or conscious "approval" 19 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited as mandated under law. The expressions "recommendation" and "approval" are conceptually distinct and cannot be treated as interchangeable. A recommendation merely suggests or advises a course of action and carries no binding force, whereas approval denotes an authoritative sanction after due application of mind. It is a settled principle of statutory interpretation that where the language of the statute is plain and unambiguous, the same has to be construed literally. In this regard, reliance is placed on the judgment of the Hon'ble Apex Court in the case of B. Premanand & Others Vs Mohan Koikal & Others Civil Appeal No. 2684 of 2007 dated 16th March 2011 wherein, the Hon'ble Court while adhering to the rule of interpretation at various place has held as follows:- It may be mentioned in this connection that the first and foremost principle of interpretation of a statute in every system of interpretation is the literal rule of interpretation. The other rules of interpretation e.g. the mischief rule, purposive interpretation etc. can only be resorted to when the plain words of a statute are ambiguous or lead to no intelligible results or if read literally would nullify the very object of the statute. Where the words of a statute are absolutely clear and unambiguous, recourse cannot be had to the principles of interpretation other than the literal rule, vide Swedish Match AB vs. Securities and Exchange Board, India , AIR 2004 SC 4219. As held in Prakash Nath Khanna vs. C.L.T. 2004 (9) SCC 686, the language employed in a statute is the determinative factor of the legislative intent. The legislature is presumed to have made no mistake. The presumption is that it intended to say what it has said. Assuming there is a defect or an omission in the words used by the legislature, the Court cannot correct or make up the deficiency. vide Delhi Financial Corporation vs. Rajiv Anand 2004 (11) SCC 625. Where the legislative intent is clear from the language, the Court should give effect to it, vide Government of Andhra Pradesh vs. Road Rollers Owners Welfare Association 2004(6) SCC 210, and the Court should not seek to amend the law in the garb of interpretation. 20 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited As observed by Lord Granworth in Grundy v. Pinniger, (1852) 1 LJ Ch 405: ""To adhere as closely as possible to the literal meaning of the words used, is a cardinal rule from which if we depart we launch into a sea of difficulties which it is not easy to fathom." In other words, once we depart from the literal rule, then any number of interpretations can be put to a statutory provision, each Judge having a free play to put his own interpretation as he likes. This would be destructive of judicial discipline, and also the basic principle in a democracy that it is not for the Judge to legislate as that is the task of the elected representatives of the people. Even if the literal interpretation results in hardship or inconvenience. it has to be followed (see G.P. Singh's Principles of Statutory Interpretations, 9th Edn. pp 45-49). Hence departure from the literal rule should only be done in very rare cases, and ordinarily there should be judicial restraint in this connection. As observed by this Court in CIT vs. Keshab Chandra Mandal , AIR 1950 SC 265 "Hardship or inconvenience cannot alter the meaning of the language employed by the Legislature if such meaning is clear on the face of-the statute" In Union of India and another vs. Hansoli Devi and others 2002(7) SCC (vide para 9), this Court observed: "It is a cardinal principle of construction of a statute that when the language of the statute is plain and unambiguous, then the court must give effect to the words used in the statute and it would not be open to the courts to adopt a hypothetical construction on the grounds that such construction is more consistent with the alleged object and policy of the Act." As such, we would like to state that there was no valid approval of the competent authority and that it was merely a recommendation to do an act leaving it on the discretion of the Assessing Officer. This defect is not curable U/s 292BC since there was no approval at all. As such, the proceedings initiated is ab-initio void and assessment framed thereby is fit to be quashed. 21 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited In the present case, the sanctioning authority has not granted any express approval as required under Section 151 of the Act. What has been recorded is merely a recommendation to proceed further, thereby leaving the ultimate discretion to the Assessing Officer. Such a defect goes to the very root of the jurisdiction assumed under Sections 147 / 148 of the Act and cannot be treated as a procedural irregularity curable under Sections 292BC of the Act. The absence of valid sanction renders the initiation of reassessment proceedings void ab initio and assessment framed by the Assessing Officer is hereby quashed by following the decision rendered by the Hon'ble Supreme Court order as in the case of CIT vs. Keshab Chandra Mandal , AIR 1950 SC 265 (supra). Further, from the reasons recorded, it is evident that no independent satisfaction was arrived at by the Assessing Officer and the reopening was initiated solely on the basis of information received from the Investigation Wing. The assessment order also reveals that no independent enquiry or verification was conducted by the Assessing Officer during the reassessment proceedings. The entire addition has been made merely on the basis of the investigation report without bringing any cogent material on record to establish that the assessee had introduced its own unaccounted money in the garb of share capital. Even on merit, it is noticed that the assessee company had allotted 9,50,000 equity shares having face value of ₹10 each at a premium of ₹40 per share to M/s Smart Tradecom Private Limited. The assessee had furnished Form No. 2, copies of Board Resolutions, share certificates, audited financial statements, bank statements and other documentary evidences in support of the transaction, which were placed in the paper book. From the material available on record, it emerges that M/s Smart Tradecom Private Limited was admittedly a group concern of the assessee company and both the companies were managed and controlled by common directors and family members. The audited financial statements 22 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited of the share applicant company reveal that the investments were made out of its own capital, reserves and surplus accumulated from earlier years. No material has been brought on record by the Revenue authorities to establish that the share applicant company had received any accommodation entries or funds from alleged shell entities during the relevant year. It is further observed that M/s Smart Tradecom Private Limited was already an existing shareholder of the assessee company from earlier years. The audited balance sheet placed on record clearly reveals that as on 31.03.2012, the said company held 96,000 shares of the assessee company and as on 31.03.2013, it held 10,46,000 shares. Thus, the shareholding relationship between the assessee company and the share applicant company was pre-existing and had already been accepted by the Revenue in earlier years. Though the principle of res judicata does not strictly apply to income-tax proceedings as held by the Hon'ble Supreme Court has held in several cases including M.M. Ipoh & Ors. vs. CIT (1968) 67 ITR 106 (SC) that: The doctrine of res judicata does not apply so as to make a decision on a question of fact or law in a proceeding for assessment in one year binding in another year. At the same time, it is equally true that the principle of consistency has also been advocated by several Hon'ble courts including the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Radhasoami Satsang vs. CIT (1992) 193 ITR 321 (SC) and the Hon'ble Bombay High Court in CIT vs. Arthur Andersen & Co. (2009) 318 ITR 229 (Bom) by holding that the decision made in earlier years is binding in subsequent years and should be followed. From the above decisions, it follows that a delicate balance needs to be maintained between the principle of consistency depending upon the facts and the governing legal position prevailing in each case. Thus, the share application money received by the assessee company from M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd has been accepted in the earlier years and that there is no change in the nature of transaction 23 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited during the year nor any specific adverse finding is drawn by the AO to allege any accommodation entry, therefore the addition made as alleged bogus share capital U/s 68 of the Act is uncalled for and deleted. The Hon'ble Supreme Court emphasized that consistency in approach should be maintained in tax proceedings unless there is any material change in facts. In the present case, the Revenue has failed to bring any fresh or adverse material on record to demonstrate that the impugned transaction represented accommodation entries or bogus share capital. Neither the name of M/s Smart Tradecomm Private Limited appears in any list of shell companies nor has any statement of any alleged entry operator been brought on record. The entire addition has been made merely on suspicion and on the basis of generalized information received from the Investigation Wing. The assessee has duly discharged the onus cast upon it under Section 68 of the Act by establishing the identity of the share applicant, the genuineness of the transaction and the creditworthiness of the investor company. The identity of the investor company is undisputed since it is a group concern managed by common directors. The genuineness of the transaction stands proved from the fact that the transactions were routed through regular banking channels and duly reflected in the audited books of account and statutory records filed before the Registrar of Companies. The creditworthiness of the investor company is also substantiated from its audited financial statements. It is further noticed that the Assessing Officer has merely disregarded the documentary evidences furnished by the assessee without pointing out any defect or discrepancy therein. No enquiry whatsoever was conducted to dislodge the evidences produced by the assessee. Such an approach is unsustainable in law. Reliance in this regard Hon'ble Jurisdictional High Court in the case of CIT Vs M/s Sidhi Vinayak Metcon Ltd in TA No. 39 of 2009 vide order dated 05/04/2018 has held as under:- 24 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited 5. Having heard counsels appearing for both sides and looking to the facts and circumstances of the case, we see no reason to entertain this Tax Appeal mainly for the reason that the addition made by the Assessing Officer should not have been made by him because amount received towards the share applications was by way of cheque. All the details regarding applicants were furnished by the assessee and Department could have issued notices to such applicants. Hence, there was no need for the Assessing Officer to make addition of Rs. 95,15,000/- 6. It appears that before the Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) also such prayer could have been made by the Department for issuance of Notices upon share applicants. No such application was preferred before C.LT (Appeals) by this appellant. Hence, we see no reason at this stage to remand the matter to the Assessing Officer for further verification of share applications. 7. It appears that similar type of cases are coming to this court often and most of the time same type of error is committed by the Assessing Officers, either deliberately or due to "Induced ignorance". This is not the first time such type of matter has been taken up by this Court It is now high time for the Commissioner, Income Tax to have orientation courses or induction courses conducted for the Assessing Officers to make them understand that whenever assessee receives any amount by cheque, there is a need for the Assessing Officer to give notice to the drawers of those cheques That from the facts of the above case , we find that the Ld. AO has vaguely alleged M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd to be a shell company whereas the fact remains neither there is any statement of any entry operator nor does the name of the company flagged in the list of shell companies prepared by the Investigation Unit. As stated above, M/s Smart Tradecomm Pvt Ltd is a group company of the assessee and was already pre-existing share holder from the previous year. That the undisputed fact is that the assessee has received share capital money from its group company complete details of which were available on record and without pointing out 25 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited any specific defect to the same, the Ld AO has made the addition in a vague manner which has been confirmed by Ld CIT(A). The provisions of section 68 is not applicable in the case of the assessee to the extent that the identity cannot be doubted as the share applicant is a group company controlled and managed by the same management. Moreover, the share applicant was already pre-existing share holder and genuineness of the transaction also cannot be doubted since the transactions are through banking channel duly reflected in the audited books of the assessee as well as share applicant, along with necessary compliances to the RoC. The Ld AO has himself stated that there was investment made in the share applicant company sometime in 2010. The said investment in the hands of the recipient being the share applicant company was accepted and that the same has been liquidated and invested in the assessee company over the years. As such, the creditworthiness of the share applicant company also stands explained. As such, we hold that the provisions of section 68 is not applicable in case of the assessee. Moreover, the audited financial statements of the assessee company also reveal that there existed a genuine business requirement for raising share capital during the relevant year. The turnover of the assessee company increased substantially from ₹64 crores in March 2012 to ₹86 crores in March 2013, thereby justifying the raising of share capital and premium from its group concern. In merit of the case and applicability of provision of section 68 of the Act, we find support from the decision rendered by the Hon'ble Supreme Court as well as Hon'ble High Court which are hereunder:- 26 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited a) Principal Commissioner of Income Tax v. Himachal Fibers Ltd - [2018] 98 taxmann.com 173 (SC) SLP Dismissed - Hon'ble Apex Court while upholding the decision of the decision of Hon'ble Delhi High Court has concluded as below:- Cash credit (Share capital) In course of assessment. Assessing Officer noted that assessee was a sick company during relevant year but had nevertheless collected substantial amounts to extent of Rs. 12 crores invested by two share applicants Assessing Officer took a view that identity of shareholders and genuineness of transaction had not been established and, accordingly, brought to tax said amount -Tribunal as well High Court found that even though assessee was a sick company earlier, yet it pulled out of woods in year 2010 - It was also noticed that identity of share applicants was clearly revealed but Assessing Officer did not conduct any enquiry except resting his conclusions on surmises. Accordingly, addition made by Assessing Officer was deleted - Whether on facts, SLP filed against order passed by High Court was to be dismissed-Held, yes [Para 2] [In favour of assessee) b) Principal Commissioner of Income-tax Central vs. Pearl Tracom (P.) Ltd. [2025] 176 taxmann.com 284 (Calcutta) [01-07-2025] wherein it has been held as follows:- 6. As noted above, the order passed pursuant to the second revisional order under section 263 has been quashed by the learned Tribunal which has been upheld by this Court. The reasons to believe for reopening the assessment has been recorded by the assessing officer and ongoing through it we find that the name of the assessee does not feature in the body of the reasons but the assessee has been shown as a beneficiary in the reasons for reopening in paragraph 'details of enquiries made'. There is no specific allegation as against the assessee and as to how layering of funds had taken place. This aspect of the matter was considered by the learned Tribunal and found that the assessing officer never disclosed the details of layers through which the alleged money has been reached into bank account of the assessee nor any information was shared in respect of any documentary evidence for the alleged 27 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited transaction of accommodation entry for which the assessee was stated to be a beneficiary. This finding of the learned Tribunal is well justified. Apart from that we also find that no specific reasons have been mentioned by the assessing officer as to the nature of the transaction of accommodation entry qua the assessee and this aspect was examined by the learned Tribunal and it was observed that the assessing officer does not disclosed as to whether it is an income or expenses or an allowance or share capital or loan, etc. The factual aspect of the regards the share capital and the share premium was examined by the assessing officer in detail and the documents and details furnished by the assessee were accepted while passing the assessment order dated 30.12.2016. The attempt to revise the said order by way of a second revisional order under section 263 proved futile as the assessee was successful before the Tribunal as well as before this Court. Thus, the learned Tribunal on considering the factual position had rightly granted relief to the assessee. The court took note of the factual position and also the decision of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in Income-tax Officer v. Lakhmani Mewal Das [1976] 103 ITR 437 (SC) wherein the Hon'ble Supreme Court held that the reasons for the formation of the belief must have a rational connection with or relevant bearing on the formation of the belief. Rational connection postulates that there must be a direct nexus or live link between the material coming to the notice of the Income-tax Officer and the formation of his belief that there has been escapement of the income of the assessee from assessment in the particular year because of his failure to disclose fully and truly all material facts. c) Principal Commissioner of Income-tax vs. Sreeleathers [2022] 143 taxmann.com 435 (Calcutta) [14-07-2022)- Further to be noted that where the assessee furnishes full details regarding the creditors, it is up to the department to pursue the matter further to locate those creditors and examine their creditworthiness. The assessee responded to the show cause notice and submitted the reply. The documents annexed to the reply were classified 28 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited under 3 categories namely: to establish the identity of the lender, to prove the genuineness of the transactions and to establish the creditworthiness of the lender. The Assessing Officer has brushed aside these documents and in a very casual manner has stated that mere filing PAN details. balance sheet does not absolve the assessee from his responsibility of proving the nature of transaction. There is no discussion by the Assessing Officer on the correctness of the stand taken by the assessee. Thus, going by the records placed by the assessee, it could be safely held that the assessee has discharged his initial burden and the burden shifts on the Assessing Officer to enquire further into the matter which he failed to do. It is not enough for the Assessing Officer to say so but he should record reasons in writing as to why the documents which were filed by the assessee along with the reply does not go to establish the identity of the lender or prove the genuineness of the transaction or establish the creditworthiness of the lender. Where Assessing Officer issued a show cause notice to assessee-company on ground that it had received unsecured loans from alleged bogus company and subsequently passed an assessment order without bringing on record any reason in writing as to why documentary evidences furnished by assessee failed to prove genuineness of said transaction and why it did not establish identity of lender, such impugned assessment order passed being utterly perverse was liable to be quashed. d) Principal Commissioner of Income-tax vs. Ambe Tex Fab (India) (P.) Ltd. [2025] 173 taxmann.com 392 (Calcutta) [07-03-2025] - The Hon'ble Kolkata High Court upholding the detailed order of the Hon'ble Tribunal has held as follows:- The issue which falls for consideration is whether the Tribunal was justified in setting aside the order passed by the Commissioner (Appeals) by which the Commissioner (Appeals) affirmed the order passed by the Assessing Officer under section 143(3) . The Tribunal 29 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited has elaborately considered the factual position and faulted the manner in which the assessment was completed by observing that it was a cryptic order without discussing the facts of the matter nor the submissions made by the assessee nor the documents produced by the assessee to prove the three factors, namely, identity, genuineness of the transactions and creditworthiness of the subscribers. The Tribunal also found that though such documents were once again produced before the Commissioner (Appeals), but the same were not referred to nor any defect or discrepancy was pointed out under the said documents. Apart from that, the Tribunal has also pointed out that there are factual mistakes by the Commissioner (Appeals) while passing the order which are contrary to the conclusion arrived at by the Assessing Officer. The Tribunal took note of the decision of the Supreme Court in Pr. CIT (Central)-1 v. NRA Iron & Steel (P.) Ltd. [2019] 103 taxmann.com 48/262 Taxman 74/412 ITR 161 (SC) and noted that the principles which were summed up in the said decision when a case is considered under section 68 . After noting the said decision, the Tribunal examined the factual position and found that the initial burden casted upon the assessee has been discharged inasmuch as the assessee had produced the documents to prove the identity of the subscribers, the genuineness of the transaction and creditworthiness of the subscribers. If such was the factual position then in terms of the order passed by the Supreme Court, the burden shifts on the department to prove the contrary. For doing so, it is necessary that the Assessing Officer should embark upon a fact findings exercise which, unfortunately, the Assessing Officer did not do in the instant case. The revenue cannot deny the fact that the order of assessment, as observed by the Tribunal, is a cryptic order. Thus, no question of law, much less substantial questions of law, arising for consideration in this appeal. Accordingly, the appeal fails and is dismissed. [Para 5] 30 ITA No.386/Ran/2025 Santpuria Alloys Private Limited e) Income-tax Officer vs. Cornerstone Property Investments (P .) Ltd. [2024] 164 taxmann.com 464 (SC)[15-07-2024]- SLP dismissed against order of High Court that where assessee allotted shares at premium to a company and assessee produced various documents including copies of investor's bank statements. copies of share certificates which showed that it had huge funds, and further, transfer of funds was made through bank, assessee had proved identity and creditworthiness of investor and genuineness of transactions and, thus, no addition under section 68 could be made on account of share premium received by assessee. We, after considering the totality of the facts and circumstances of the case, it is held that the reassessment proceedings initiated under Sections 147 / 148 of the Act are bad in law and void ab initio for want of valid approval under Section 151 of the Act. It is further held that the addition made under Section 68 / 69A of the Act is unsustainable since the assessee had satisfactorily established the identity, genuineness and creditworthiness of the share applicant company and the Revenue has failed to bring any adverse material on record to rebut the evidences furnished by the assessee. Accordingly, the assessment order passed by the Assessing Officer and sustained by the learned CIT(A) is set aside and the addition of ₹4,75,00,000 made for assessment year 2013-14 is hereby deleted. The appeal of the assessee stands allowed. 8. In the result, appeal of the assessee is allowed. Kolkata, the 22nd May, 2026.
